sentences.text: [NON-ENGLISH], it was named after a German general.
And believe me, we didn't pick this ship because it was the only way out, but we had to take a German ship, because of the money situation.
The passengers on the ship didn't really treat us any-- any way, I mean, like strangers.
We made-- we-- we stuck with our own Jewish people that were like we.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was not too much contact, except in case of some of them made business with it by buying the ship money.
You could buy radios, cameras on the ship, and so on.
You could buy a Leica camera.
I know if you heard that name, but it was at one time one of the most expensive cameras.
You could buy those on the-- on the ship.
So they-- they made a good deal.
Yeah, they didn't give us-- naturally, they made money on the exchange rate.
But that I can't really blame them, you know.
They did, nevertheless, did us a favor.
And we had some extra money in our hands of like English pounds, or German, or American dollars.
30 Days, or 31 days.
I'm not sure.
Well 31 days, I think.
Because we went the wrong way.
We had to go from Bremerhaven through-- through-- through the Gulf of Biscay to Gibraltar, Mediterranean, to the Suez Canal-- the long way.
Now, the Italian ships took off in Genoa, and they did-- they had a lot shorter trip.
Took-- took almost a week to go to Genoa.
We stopped two days in Rotterdam and so on, because they took on loads of aluminum.
And in Egypt and Port Said, they took on onions.
I never forgot that either-- the smell.
And what was the reason that they went a long way?
Well the Germans had no opening in the Mediterranean, no harbor, I mean.
The German ships all left either from Hamburg, or Bremen, or Kiel.
We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had a little bit of money.
There was an international fund.
It was basically German Jews established that fund.
And we got $50 per person.
In Rotterdam, we got it.
We went ashore there.
There was a Jewish community center, or whatever it was-- they had that money for us.
There was an address.
And so we had a little bit of money.
And my uncle sent me some money.
One uncle that was in France sent some money to Shanghai to the-- it was like American Express.
But it was actually-- the only address we had, it was actually address of the shipping company, which had an office there.
It was similar to American Express.
This were also where we got the mail in the beginning.
I really forgot the name of the place.
And so-- so that was the first thing we did.
So we had some money.
And there was a friend of-- my uncle's friend in France, too, he was recommended to us.
He was supposed to meet at the ship.
He was at the wrong side, so he didn't.
So we had that one night-- one night at the-- at the camp, which was an old military barracks turned into a refugee camp kind of.
And it was a-- it was a pretty bad night, especially for the women. 100 people in one room.
And some people who lived there before were not exactly very friendly, some of them.
And my-- we were only one night there.
The next morning, my father-- my father wasn't that well.
He was in the military and he was in a war before.
It didn't shock him as much, shocked him, too.
I didn't know what to make of it.
[LAUGHS] The next day we moved in for temporary in this friend of a friend of my uncle's house.
He had a little house he rent.
He rented a store downstairs out.
He had a big room upstairs.
He lived there with a friend.
And he had two small rooms, which we-- which we left, which we got.
But he-- he didn't have a bathroom.
But across the street was a park.
A park and it was patronized usually by, particularly by a lot of foreigners, particularly by the English.
They were playing bocce, lawn bowling there on weekends and so on.
That park had toilets, water closets, you know.
And he said, you buy for one Chinese dollars you buy-- buy a pass for that.
And you can use the bathrooms there.
Only thing is, they were closed from evening, 10:00 to 6:00 in the morning, or something like that.
But this was no good.
We moved out of there pretty fast.
And we moved to another little rooming house for refugees.
They-- some people had some money.
So they leased these houses from the Chinese.
You can lease them for 99 years or shorter.
You cannot buy them.
And then they turned them into like a rooming house, where they rented room to a lot of different people.
This is what this man did.
And this was the first place where we lived for about a year.
And it was-- it was not that bad, when you consider everything.
You lived in one room with your parents.
You had your own privacy.
And the bathrooms were-- they had even water closets.
They had cesspools, but nevertheless there were toilet-- regular toilets.
And they had some electric heather there, there was some heating.
That first winter was very cold.
It snowed there.
It normally doesn't.
And so we had a kitchen community with electric stoves.
It wasn't that bad.
But after a year, we couldn't afford it anymore.
And we moved to a cheaper place, which didn't have all that.
But we had actually a little nicer room.
But you had a Chinese toilet for yourself, which was just a bucket they picked up in the morning.
And we lived there till-- till-- sometime-- sometime early or in the middle of 1942, when we couldn't afford that anymore.
Then we got into a camp.
But that was difficult to get into a camp.
We needed-- we needed somebody's help.
They were some wealthy Jews in Shanghai who gave a lot of money.
And we found out through a distant cousin of my mother, he told us about that man.
He said, he gives a lot of money.
He had a lot of influence.
If you go to him, you will be able to get from-- they called it kitchen fund at that time, at least a meal a day or two meals a day at a time.
And they would get you in the camp.
My father saw that man.
His name was Newman.
He was a manager of a company called [NON-ENGLISH], who was a Swiss-- they were-- they did-- they made cloth, wool, I think, material and something like that.
It was a big company.
And my father thought, the man was very nice.
And he helped us to get into the camp.
And get also some food, because they try to take it away from the people.
They were short on funds that they didn't live in camps.
And we couldn't even pay the rent anymore.
In Shanghai?
We lived always in a closed area, in the area called Hongkew.
And the part of Hongkew that was bombed out in 1937, and people started to build up again.
Some of the Russians build it.

There were some Russians who build it up again, and released those houses they must have leased, like their first man where across from the park, he leased it from a Russian, who lived actually there.
I met this man.
And if my father would have used all the money we had, we could have maybe done that.
And maybe it would have been better.
But he didn't want to get rid of the last $100 we had.
Or we had $150, $175, something like that.
This would have been $100 for that house.
I remember that.
And this Russian he did, this kid, he was in business doing that.
And other Russians did, too.
And some refugees, themselves, leased it from the Chinese.
The Jewish community?
Our Jewish community?
[SIGHS] I would say, tense.
I don't know a better word.
Some people were awful nice, some were not.
Just like everybody else.
It was a community like everything.
But the tension, there was a certain amount of a cloud hanging over us.
And even though it was not-- couldn't be easily defined, the threat was there.
We knew the Nazis were in Shanghai, too.
There were Nazi flags, and so on.
We knew that much.
But as far as I'm concerned, we younger people, we were not that unhappy there.
We're talking now about the older people.
We-- we-- we got used to those things, like the dead on the street, dead children they put out in the street to be picked up, dead people in the street to be picked up by the city.
And-- they called them [NON-ENGLISH], the ones that picked up those pots every day.
And people lived on the street, in the summer particularly.
It was hot in their houses.
They were crowded, the Chinese now.
And I don't think it took me very long to not seeing what I didn't want to see.
This is about-- this is about all.
I saw it.
And it-- but it was none of my concern.
My mother had a harder time.
My father had less of a hard time.
And a lot of people had a-- some people took like duck to the water, they didn't mind it at all.
They figured out a way to make a living.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But not many, I would say-- not too many.
And some gave up.
There were those who gave up, even younger people, especially professionals that had absolutely no field there.
Like you take attorneys, particular attorneys-- even people who were judges once.
They were somebody once.
Some of them could never accept it.
I met in a camp, we lived in a-- when I lived in a camp, we lived-- 30 boys, from seven to 20, in the same room, double beds.
And we didn't-- it was nice.
But after a while, they needed this room for some older people.
They mixed them in.
Older men in there.
It was a dumb move, but somebody was in charge to do it.
And some of these older people, first of all they were not clean.
They were very unhappy people.
Single older men.
And under this-- I mean, I cannot really find fault with them, except that they were difficult, and they didn't get along with young people.
There were exceptions.
There was one man who took up-- who took some kids that he saw it where the parents really couldn't help them, like one fellow in there, and helped them with their school work and so on.
There was one man, his name was Kirney.
Never forget that.
And I met-- I met the one boy he particularly kind of adopted, because his father wasn't all there, evidently from concentration camp.
The mother with a very uneducated woman, they had three children.
He was the oldest.
And I met this man.
He did fine.
He did fight in life, [LAUGHS] thanks to the man-- thanks to that man.
But there was another man in there, for instance, he'd never take a bath.
He never take a shower-- you could take shower.
There were community showers.
He never-- he was filthy.
But he cleaned always his fingernails.
Never forget that.
He was a man maybe no more than 42, 43.
And he had a sister in another room that they said it was the same thing.
This man was a living encyclopedia.
He was absolutely brilliant.
I'm not sure what he was before.
He wouldn't talk about it.
He was somebody.
But he had lost his mind, my opinion, both of them, he and his sister had lost their mind.
He was the most educated man I've ever met.
There was no doubt about him being not only educated, but brilliant.
Tragedy.
No.
I don't.
Don't know what happened for him.
I don't know.
As far as I know, he didn't speak what he was doing, didn't say what he was doing.
He was somebody.
I mean, he was somebody outstanding.
He could have been a college professor.
He could have been a judge.
I don't know.
But if you ever ask him, I'm not sure either about it.
When you talk to him very, very, very friendly, very nice.
And he would give you-- and you could ask him questions.
He knew so many things.
There was no question about it-- he knew.
And he had a very, very good education.
And he had a very brilliant mind.
His brilliant mind was still working.
But being like he was, otherwise, act like a vegetable.
And I come to the only conclusion, he did lose his mind, you know?
He was not a raving maniac, but he gave up to the degree that he didn't talk about his past.
You said people were dying on the street.
Oh, the Chinese?
Oh, a lot of beggars were dying on the street, yes.

But the Chinese, I asked, worked-- where I worked, I always worked, where I worked was a Chinese office employee where I worked in the factory, owned by a German, who was married to a Jewish woman, who-- and had an adopted son who was Jewish.
And he hired a lot of us to do work.
But we did the same work that the Chinese did in his little factory.
And I talked to the office man, and office man, whatever he was, office boy, whatever they called him, but he was really a fairly educated man.
I said, why do the people put that on the street?
He said, they can't afford a funeral.
The Chinese funeral is expensive.
They pick it up put and put it on the street, I haven't got the money to pay for it.
To be religious here is very expensive, he told me.
And the religious funeral is evidently required, unless the city pick them up and burn them in a crematorium.
That's what they did.
That was my first job.
I worked there two years.
They made cigarette cases, lipstick containers, aluminum products.
He dyed aluminum and so on.
He used to work in construction.
He used to make extruded aluminum for-- for high rise buildings.
But something went wrong there, too, and he-- I don't know, maybe with the war breaking out in 1937 there, he started from scratch doing something like that.
He sold these-- these were give-away items he sold to large companies.
Some of them were German.
Because we put-- I remember one was Siemens, actually.
He put stamp in there, and they were giving them away.
It was sent all over the world, I suppose.
And then he got a large contract, a try-out contract with Woolworth's in the United States.
And it-- when-- when-- he-- It would have put him really back up, high up again.
There were millions of toy trumpets and toy saxophones.
And he went from aluminum then to tin.
And we worked on those.
And the trial order was 40,000 or something like that.
It was a pretty good trial order.
And then the war broke out.
And this was over for him, too.
Weidner, W-E-I-D-N-E-R.
He lived in Asia for a long time, evidently.
I believe he lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
He was a man about 60 at the time.
He was not obviously a Nazi, because his first wife was Jewish.
His son was Jewish.
His adopted son was Jewish at that time in his early 20s.
Weidner Construction Company.
I even have a letter of recommendation from him, because he did originally constructing work, constructing work-- but he didn't do it when I was there.
Well, first of all, I lost my job.
But-- and a lot of other people did.
The British and Americans, who didn't heed the advice from their own governments, and hadn't left, were arrested and put in-- in-- put in camps, you know.
Prisoners-- not prisoners-- what do you call it?
In-- they were interned, actually, you know, by the Japanese.
And life-- and the Shanghai International settlement was no international settlement.
Where overnight, the Japanese occupied it, with their troops.
The French sector stayed French sector, because they had an allegiance with the Vichy government, who were, at that time, kind of an ally of the Nazis and Japanese.
The immediate change was, for one thing, the streetcars were stopped at the bridge-- at the bridges over the river, Hongkew, the other side of the river.
They were moved there.
We had better transportation at the moment.
But the chance of a livelihood was going down.
Almost all the companies, people that work for British and American companies, there were evidently drastic changes or they were closed.
I am not too familiar with it, but they were-- most of them were arrested.
That were some people that were not-- for instance, the Kadoorie brothers were not arrested.
They were interned in their own castle-like home, called Marble Hall.
It was a marble hall.
I've seen it-- it was like a castle.
They had to pay so much per day, so the Japanese could guard them.
They had to pay for the guards.
But they didn't put them in any camps.
That I know.
Well, the Kadoories were-- particularly one brother of them-- of them-- was a big philanthropist, and he helped the refugees a lot.
He built-- for instance, he built a Jewish school, and he paid for it.
And he was-- he did a lot for the-- for the refugees, particular the children, previous to that.
I don't know if he could help anything during those war years, before he did.
Horace Kadoorie, I think was his name, Horace.
The camps were-- were usually existing-- these are schools, or-- or schools where-- you know, where the kids had room and board.
Like they camp I stayed in, was, I believe it was affiliated with the Red Cross.
It was affiliated-- it was an American-type school, where the kids lived-- where the kids lived in there.
I imagine a lot of them, or most of them were Chinese, nevertheless.
And half the buildings were bombed out and torn down.
Some of them with still some bricks left over, and so on.
The other half, we lived in there.
And we lived about between 800 and 900 people in that camp.
It was a park-like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] area.
It was about a quarter mile long.
It was about a little wider than a football field, because there was a soccer field which went across-- well, not all the way across the property.
So I figure the width of a football field, a little bit more.
Than a quarter mile length.
And with a lot of empty areas, because the buildings were gone, and a lot of areas that were park-like.

And they had to take, the Jewish community, whoever took it over-- you know, the joint committee, or whatever they called it, kitchen fund, they had different names-- they had to keep the gardener and a-- and a-- and an Indian caretaker who lived there with his wife and children.
They had to pay them basically to keep the property in good shape, the outside yards, which they-- which they did.
Yes.
Yes, it was Chaoufoong street.
C-H-A-O-U-F-O-O-N-G. 680.
I know how to spell it now, after I looked it up.
No, no, no.
I stayed in a room with 30 boys.
There were 15 double beds, 30 boys.
I know that.
And when it became-- when-- we had to keep it clean, and we did.
We did like the other.
I was in the upper berth, and the other one-- a boy a couple years younger than I-- was in the lower berth.
We cleaned it out.
And we got the mattress to be cleaned.
I forgot how we got the mattress cleaned really.
I think they may have helped us with that.
But the bed, itself, bed bugs got in there.
We burned them out.
Then put them in cups with water in there, so they wouldn't get up in the bed any more.
It worked.
But when the several older men came in, and when I found out that some people had lice, then I-- then I-- then I asked my parents, can't you get me out, so I stay with you?
And my parents stayed in another building where there were families.
And they got me out.
I didn't want to stay in there, anymore.
I figure I don't need that here.
The kids were clean.
The older people that-- that's why I say, a lot of them had probably lost some of their normal facilities.
Like I said, there was at least one man in there who was wonderful with the kids.
And he was also clean on himself.
His name was Kirney.
Never forget his name.
Wonderful man.
But others were different.
And when I found out about the lice, I had enough, because you don't need it there.
They carry all kind of diseases.
And I got out.
I was lucky.
And in the other room, it was-- you lived family.
They divided the rooms up with curtains.
We were in a school building, which was brand new before the war broke out.
It was a regular, old-fashioned type school building, like they used to build them-- three floors, three classrooms on one side, two on the other.
The stairs, and some conference room on the sides.
And bathrooms-- there were bathrooms there.
But water did not go up in the second or third floor in the daytime.
There wasn't enough pressure.
But in the night it did go.
It did run.
In the camp?
Primarily Jewish, yeah, except for the intermarriage.
And some were not of Jewish faith, but they were Jewish-- Jewish considered by Hitler, because they were born Jewish.
And it didn't make too-- seem to make too much difference.
When you lived in the camp, you lived in a community.
You had to get along.
And I must say, people didn't do bad at all.
Well, first of all, I worked and worked pretty hard.
When I came home, frankly, I very often just took a book and read.
I didn't talk about anything.
But on the weekend, when we did have a day off, usually Sunday, we went out with some friends.
We went to movies, even in our restricted area we had movie houses.
There wasn't much else you can do, but on weekends they had soccer teams.
They had a whole league.
And in the camp I lived, I could literally look from my bed, from an upper berth, and could watch the game if I wanted to.
But I mean, people came in there.
There was sometimes 2,000, 3,000 people there, watching soccer games.
We played against-- I did not play soccer.
But they had some good soccer teams.
And actually, their first team, usually, they got-- there were some money for them.
That they got paid.
I heard them, myself, saying that, admitting that.
And they got-- people bought them lunches and dinners and so on.
They treated them well.
But the younger teams, of course, were usually young people.
They had-- you know-- they had like a major and a minor league.
They were just-- they were not paid anything.
But they kept us busy.
And every weekend, there was-- there was at least one or two soccer games.
And they played there two leagues, all refugees, but they also played against sometimes some foreigners, especially after-- especially after the war was over-- the Russians and Chinese.
And a lot of people used this area, those who had babies and so on, as a park, which it was.
The people who didn't live there, Sundays they came, which was kind of pleasant.
I thought that was nice.
Other people may have the same opinion.
In this camp, there were close to 900.
Yes, we had communications, still.
I don't know when it was almost impossible to have them.
The first two years we had communications with families.
We, for instance, knew that some of our relatives made it in '41 out to America.
And we didn't know-- we didn't know about the others.
And I had a correspondence with a good friend of mine.
He sat for years next to me in school, in the Jewish school.
So we were good friends.
His mother was still in Germany.
His father had passed away.
And he was in England, then he was taken prisoner of war.
Then he joined the army.
They sent him to Australia, and then to Canada finally.
And he wrote me to write his mother, because I still had- I could write still to Germany-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before the war broke out in the Pacific.
